coredata.dc:title: Tuning of the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of nitrogen doped carbon dots: A facile approach towards high efficient lubricant nanoadditives
coredata.dc:creator: Shang, Wangji
Ye, Mengting
Cai, Tao
Zhao, Lina
Zhang, Yunxiao
Liu, Dan
Liu, Shenggao
coredata.dc:description: Nanosizing is considered as a promising approach for achieving better dispersibility of nanoparticles in mediums, while the regulation of their hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity is very important issue for wide application of nanoparticles. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with hydrophilicity was prepared via namely “bottom-up” and exhibited excellent excitation-dependent maximum fluorescence at 480/560 nm, while a novel kind of liquid-like N-CDs with hydrophobicity was synthesized followed via covalent grafting stratagem and exhibited excellent excitation-dependent maximum fluorescence at 500/580 nm. Both hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs exhibited favorable dispersion stability in polar and apolar mediums respectively and were used for the first time as friction-reducing and antiwear nanoadditives in selected PEG and PAO synthetic base oils for steel/steel contact, which offered great potential in lubricant field as demonstrated for the highly efficient carbon dots nano-additives in reducing both undesirable friction and wear.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity
Lubricant nanoadditives
Tribological performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of hydrophilic N-CDs
Preparation and characterization of hydrophobic N-CDs
Photoluminescence properties and thermal stability of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs
Dispersibility and long-term stability of N-CDs in polar and apolar mediums
Application of N-CDs based nanoadditives for lubrication tests

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21404114 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grand No. 2014M561797 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2015A610051 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b, c) TEM imagine of the hydrophilic N-CDs.
(a) Survey and fine XPS structure spectra of (b) C1s and (c) N1 s of hydrophilic N-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of (a) hydrophilic N-CDs, (b) oleyl amine and (c) hydrophobic N-CDs.
Photoluminescence emission spectra of (a) hydrophilic N-CDs and (b) hydrophobic N-CDs.

Digital images of dispersion of (a) different concentration hydrophilic N-CDs in PEG base oil, (b) different concentration hydrophobic N-CDs in PAO base oil, (c) hydrophilic N-CDs in H2O, DMF and hydrophobic N-CDs in toluene, petroleum ether.

(a) Friction coefficient curves, (b) mean friction coefficient and mean wear volume of hydrophilic N-CDs in PEG with various concentrations (load: 392 N; temperature 75 °C; rotate speed: 1200 rpm; duration: 60 min).

(a) Friction coefficient curves, (b) mean friction coefficient and mean wear volume of hydrophobic N-CDs in PAO with various concentrations (load: 392 N; temperature 75 °C; rotate speed: 1200 rpm; duration: 60 min).

3D images of worn scar (a) pure PEG base oil, (b) PEG with 1.0 wt% IL, (c) PEG with 1.0 wt% hydrophilic N-CDs, (d) pure PAO base oil, (e) PAO with 1.0 wt% ZDDP and (f) PAO with 1.0 wt% hydrophobic N-CDs.
SEM imagine and elemental mapping of worn area lubricated by (a) pure PAO base oil, (b) PAO with 1.0 wt% ZDDP and (c) PAO with 1.0 wt% hydrophobic N-CDs.
Curve-fitted XPS spectra of the worn surface lubricated by PAO with 1.0 wt% hydrophobic N-CDs. ((a) Fe2p, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (e) O1s).
Raman point spectrum of the wear scar of PAO and hydrophobic N-CDs.

Raman mapping of the worn scar on the steel ball after the lubrication test using hydrophobic N-CDs in the PAO base oil. The Raman spectra corresponding to different color in the Raman mapping revealed the distribution variation of the hydrophobicity N-CDs on the worn scar.
Schematic diagram of the four-ball tester configuration used.
The surface elemental content of worn area lubricated by (a) pure PAO base oil, (b) PAO with 1.0 wt% ZDDP and (c) PAO with 1.0 wt% hydrophobic N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Tuning of the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of nitrogen doped carbon dots: A facile approach towards high efficient lubricant nanoadditives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, No. 1219, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo City, Zhejiang Province 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, No. 99, Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanosizing is considered as a promising approach for achieving better dispersibility of nanoparticles in mediums, while the regulation of their hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity is very important issue for wide application of nanoparticles. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with hydrophilicity was prepared via namely “bottom-up” and exhibited excellent excitation-dependent maximum fluorescence at 480/560 nm, while a novel kind of liquid-like N-CDs with hydrophobicity was synthesized followed via covalent grafting stratagem and exhibited excellent excitation-dependent maximum fluorescence at 500/580 nm. Both hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs exhibited favorable dispersion stability in polar and apolar mediums respectively and were used for the first time as friction-reducing and antiwear nanoadditives in selected PEG and PAO synthetic base oils for steel/steel contact, which offered great potential in lubricant field as demonstrated for the highly efficient carbon dots nano-additives in reducing both undesirable friction and wear.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demands of energy-saving and emission-reduction, much more attentions focused on the high performance and eco-friendly lubricants that enhanced the durability and efficiency of machines and vehicles as much as possible [1]. The additives is the most important and indispensable components in lubricants and anti-wear agents and friction-reducing agents are considered as the key roles in protecting the machines and enhancing the fuel economy [2]. A great amount of organic compounds, included phosphorus, sulphur, chloride and nitrogen etc., have been successfully used as lubricant additives performing with anti-wear and friction-reducing effects, however, the vast majority of being used additives would be reduced even replaced in future due to increasing strict environmental concerns [3]. It is of great interests to develop energy-saved lubricants with superior multifunctional properties in terms of reducing both friction and wear and without or lower emissions [4].

Recently researches have suggested the nanoparticles could behave as next generation additives in terms of anti-wear and friction-reducing agents owing to their low-cost, non-toxicity and high performance natures [5–12]. Classic spherical, tube and layered nanoparticles included fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene as well as their derivatives have been most studied and used as additives or solid lubricants owing to their unique self-lubricating effects [13–15]. However, their synthesis is costly and time-consuming, more importantly, the concerns of the dispersibility and long term storage stability of their suspensions are still crucial issues that needed to be resolved for future application.

Nanosizing is a promising approach for achieving better dispersibility of nanoparticles in various mediums [16, 17]. As a new kind of carbon nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have a special spherical structure and an extreme small diameter of less than 10 nm, which have rapidly attracted great research owing to their simple synthesis, high water solubility, excellent photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and so on [18–20]. It is noted that the CQDs showed excellent solubility in aqueous (polar) mediums, and several CQDs nanocomposites with hydrophilicity derived from singular carbon source and ionic liquid have been successfully developed and applied as additives in polar mediums or lubricants such as PEG synthetic oil in ours and others' works [21–25], however, the pristine CQDs with optimized structure as additives has not been exploited. Moreover, in consideration of itself facile surface functionalization nature, the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of CQDs could be tuned by grafting with target molecules or groups, thus achieving a stable and long-term CQDs nanoparticles dispersion in non-aqueous (apolar) medium. So the hydrophobic CQDs showed potential for being used as nanoadditive in base oils (apolar medium), and corresponding evaluation of its tribological properties could be exploited and evaluation.

For practical purposes, the tuning of hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of CQDs is of great significance and high interest. In order to realize mild synthetic condition and enhanced tribological properties, a kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared by a low-temperature solid-phase method [26–32]. Urea was chosen due to its low-cost, abundance and nitrogen-rich nature, which could realize the solid-phase reaction to proceed at a lower temperature and shorter time. Then the hydrophobic N-CDs were achieved by pose-modification of the as-prepared hydrophilic carbon dots using covalent grafting method. The as-obtained two kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots were characterized with HR-TEM, FT-IR, XRD and XPS. Afterward, the hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots dispersion with polar synthetic base oil of PEG as medium was obtained, while hydrophobic nitrogen-doped carbon dots dispersion with apolar synthetic base oil of PAO as medium was obtained as well, and both dispersion exhibited long-term stability over 1 month. Then their tribological properties were both investigated for steel/steel contact under boundary condition in this work. The morphologies and lubrication mechanism were investigated in combination of SEM-EDS, XPS and Raman spectroscopy.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%), urea (AR grade), oleyl amine (AR, grade) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). Polyethylene glycol (PEG) with an average molecular weight of 190–210 g·mol−1 (AR grade) was also obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China) and used as the polar synthetic base oil. Commercial available polyalphaolefin (PAO) was purchased from China National Offshore Oil Corporation and used as apolar synthetic base oil. Organic solvents included toluene, petroleum ether, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was obtained from Sinopham Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China) in AR grade. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. In addition, the bis(salicylato)borate ionic liquid (IL) [33] with environmental friendliness nature was synthesized according to our previous works and used as reference for hydrophilic N-CDs, while zinc dialkyldithiophosphates (ZDDP) was purchased from commercial channels and used as reference for hydrophobic N-CDs.

3.607 g (0.06 mol) of urea and 3.848 g (0.02 mol) of citric acid mixed in an agate mortar and grounded to a uniform powder. Afterward, a 100 ml round flask was placed into a silicone oil bath at room temperature and then the oil bath was heated to 180 °C, following, the mixed powder was added into the flask and reacted 30 min for dehydration and carbonization. The resultant green products was purified by alternative washing with ethanol and centrifugation at 12, 000 rpm for three times, and then dialyzing against pure water through a dialysis membrane for 24 h, and the water was removed using a rotary evaporator and the hydrophilic N-CDs samples were obtained.

Four 100 ml round flask was placed into a silicone oil bath. After the oil bath was heated to 130 °C, 1.0 g of hydrophilic N-CDs and 1.0, 2.5, 5.0 and 10.0 g of oleylamine was respectively added into the different flasks with toluene as solvent, and the reaction mixture was heated and refluxed for 6 h and finally the hydrophobic N-CDs samples were obtained.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on a HRTEM JEOL2100 system operating at 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a Bruker Axs D8 Discover using Cu Ka radiation range from 5o~60o. XPS were acquired on a PHI-5702 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of as-synthesized hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs were recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermofisher Nicolet 6700). Fluorescence spectra were collected F-7000 through a Fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Lumina). Thermo-gravimetric measurements were performed by a Mettler Toledo TGA thermo-gravimetric instrument, carried out from 50 to 600 °C with the heating rate of 10 °C/min under the nitrogen flow. The morphologies and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectra of worn surfaces were obtained on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss EVO18). 3D image were measured by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM700). Raman spectra were recorded using a Renishaw Invia Reflex spectrometer (Renishaw, England).

The friction-reducing and anti-wear of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs were tested on a four-ball tester (Tianji, Xiamen, China), which was performed in a ball-on-ball configuration by oscillating an GCr15 steel ball over three identical balls with a diameter of 12.7 mm, HRC of 59–61 and roughness of less than 0.032 μm under boundary lubrication (rotating rate, 1200 rpm; duration, 60 min; temperature 75 °C; load, 392 N). After tests, the wear scar diameter (WSD) of the three lower balls was measured using an optical microscope, while the friction coefficients were recorded automatically by a computer using a data-acquiring system linked to the friction and wear tester. (See Scheme 1 .)

To estimate the wear volume for the balls after the tribology tests, we used the following equation
V =
π h
6
3
d 2
4
+
h 2
where
h = r −
r 2
−
d 2
4
d is wear scar diameter and r is the radius of the ball [5].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hydrophilic N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 , XPS spectrum was employed to characterize the chemical composition of N-CDs, which showed three main peaks at 288.8 eV, 401.1 eV and 532.7 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1 s, and O1s, respectively. The fine N1 s spectrum of N-CDs can be fitted into two peak components with binding energies at 399.7 eV and 400.7 eV, respectively, which belong to the C-N-C and N-(C)3, respectively. The fine C1s spectrum can be fitted into four peak components with binding energies at 284.7 eV, 285.4 eV, 288.1 eV and 288.8 eV, which belong to CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively. In addition oxygen rich groups were observed on the prepared hydrophilic N-CDs sample as well, which belong to the surface functional groups [37–39]. The positions of XPS peaks indicate that the as-synthesized N-CDs are surface functionalizable owing to multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hydrophobic N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For practical purposes, the tuning of hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of N-CDs is of great significance and high interest. In comparison with hydrophilic N-CDs, the hydrophobic N-CDs is more needed owing to its application in engine oil in related to the larger number of vehicles and machines. More importantly, in consideration of zinc dialkyldithiophosphates (ZDDP) is most successful anti-wear and anti-friction additives ever invented in engine oil, while most efforts have involved in exploring new additives for replacing ZDDP that containing phosphorous and sulphur because of environmental legislation and health care norms. However, the exploitation of carbon dots nanoparticles as alternative additive for ZDDP in base oils in terms of friction-reducing and wear-resistance have not been exploited yet. Hence, the synthesis and evaluation of hydrophobic N-CDs in apolar base oils is of great significance and needed. Moreover, in order to illustrate the performance of hydrophobic N-CDs as much as possible, and balance the cost and environmental concern, oleyl amine was selected due to its low-cost, environmentally friendly nature, and strong coordinating effect for stabilizing the surface of the particles. Thus, the hydrophobic N-CDs was synthesized through covalent grafting of target molecule of oleyl amine on the surface of hydrophilic N-CDs. In consideration of the strong polar of hydrophilic N-CDs, the weight ratio of oleyl amine to hydrophilic N-CDs varied from 1:1 to 10:1, and the optimal hydrophobic N-CDs obtained when the ratios reached to 5:1.

The structure of the hydrophobic N-CDs was characterized by the FI-IR in Fig. 4 . For comparison, the oleyl amine and hydrophilic N-CDs were employed to better identify the surface functional groups of hydrophobic N-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of hydrophilic N-CDs exhibits strong and broad absorption bands at 3362 and 3237 cm−1, which are assigned to OH and NH stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of a great amount of amino and hydroxyl groups on the hydrophilic N-CDs surface. The band centred at 1678 and 1402 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN stretching vibrations, while the peak centred at 1180 cm−1 involves the CC bond and the peak at 1053 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of CO bond. According to the above results, there are oxygen- and nitrogen- containing groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and amine in hydrophilic N-CDs, endowing a hydrophilic surface on N-CDs to highly disperse in aqueous solution. After pose-modified by oleyl amine for synthesis of hydrophobic N-CDs, the characteristic vibration peak such as OH and NH in hydrophilic N-CDs decreased sharply and typical peaks such as CH2 , CO and CN are found, indicating the oleyl amine have been successfully covalently connected onto the carbon cores of hydrophilic N-CDs through amidation reaction thus a kind of liquid-like hydrophobic N-CDs obtained [40, 41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties and thermal stability of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in PL spectra (Fig. 5 ), the hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs both exhibited excellent excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission, with maximum excitation (λex) and emission wavelengths (λem) at 480 nm and 560 nm for hydrophilic CDs and 500 nm and 580 nm for hydrophobic N-CDs, respectively. The maximum wavelengths of both λex and λem for the hydrophobic N-CDs exhibit a 20 nm red-shift in comparison with those for the hydrophilic N-CDs. According to previous reports, it can be concluded that the hydrophobic N-CDs has been capped by non-polar oleylamine possessing a slight larger size, thus contributing to this 20 nm red-shift [42, 43].

The thermal stability and relative mass fraction of the grafting chemical groups on N-CDs were determined by TGA under nitrogen atmosphere. As shown in Fig. S1, N-CDs without the grafting chemical groups exhibits sharply weight loss at ~200 °C due to the decomposition of the oxygen-containing groups on the surface with a thermal residue fraction of ~29.1 wt% up to 600 °C. In the same temperature range, the oleyl amine can be totally decomposed while the N-CDs with the grafting chemical groups of oleyl amine exhibits obviously enhanced thermal stability and different decomposition behavior with a thermal residue fraction of ~3.51 wt%, which further indicate the oleyl amine have been successfully grafted onto the N-CDs. According to the results, the mass fraction of grafted oleyl amine on N-CDs is 87.9 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersibility and long-term stability of N-CDs in polar and apolar mediums
ce:section.ce:para: The optical images shown in Fig. 6 demonstrated that both hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs could be directly dissolved in various solvents and synthetic base oils, which both exhibited a long term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation. For instance, hydrophilic N-CDs with strong polarity could be dispersed well in polar mediums such as aqueous solution and organic solvent of DMF as well as synthetic base oil of PEG, while hydrophobic N-CDs with weak polarity could be dispersed well in apolar mediums such as organic solvent of toluene and petroleum ether as well as synthetic base oil of PAO.

In order to further investigate the stability of hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs dispersions in PEG and PAO base oils, UV absorbance of the samples with 1.0 wt% concentrations was measured using a UV/Vis spectrometer, as shown in Fig. S2. According to curves, it can be seen that the absorbance of two dispersions do not change obviously before and after storing 30 days, indicating that the N-CDs could be dispersed well in PEG and PAO base oils. Their stable dispersions of hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs in PEG and PAO respectively were then used for tribological evaluation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs based nanoadditives for lubrication tests
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the tribological performance of the synthesized hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs, both N-CDs as nanoadditive with different concentration in PEG and PAO base oils were tested under boundary lubrication, respectively. Friction coefficient curves, mean friction coefficient and mean wear volume were obtained using a four-ball model for steel/steel contact.

The Fig. 7 showed the mean friction coefficient and mean wear volumes as a function with different concentrations of hydrophilic N-CDs in PEG. In addition, to further illustrate the effect of the nanoadditives, we select a kind of bis(salicylato)borate ionic liquid (IL) with environmental friendliness nature and excellent tribological properties that has demonstrated in our and others' works as reference [33]. The line represented the friction coefficient of the PEG with hydrophilic N-CDs indicated the nanolubricant experienced a running-in period and then a stable friction-reducing process within tested time at 1.0 wt% additives, importantly, the friction coefficients of PEG with hydrophilic N-CDs decreased sharply with the increase of concentration up to 1.0 wt% and were obviously lower than that of pure PEG base oil, while the wear volumes showed similar trend with the friction coefficient. The mean friction coefficient and wear volumes of PEG with 1.0 wt% content of hydrophilic N-CDs are 0.0295 and 0.966 × 10−3 mm3, which corresponds to reduce of 75.9% and 82.8% in comparison with that of PEG. Likewise, the friction coefficients and wear volumes of PEG with hydrophilic N-CDs are also lower than that of PEG with 1.0 wt% content of ionic liquid, which corresponds to reduction of 44.3% and 62.9% in maximum.

On the other aspect, the mean friction coefficient and mean wear volumes as a function with different concentrations of hydrophobic N-CDs in PAO base oil were investigated and the ZDDP used in our previous work was selected as reference in Fig. 8 [44]. It is found that hydrophobic N-CDs as nanoadditive in apolar PAO base oil also exhibit superior friction-reducing (lower friction coefficients) and antiwear (smaller wear volumes) performance in comparison with that of pure PAO, which corresponds to reduce of 47.1% and 90.5% in maximum. In order to better confirm tribological behavior of hydrophobic N-CDs in PAO, the friction coefficient and wear volumes in presence of 1.0 wt% hydrophobic N-CDs in PAO have been compared with those of 1.0 wt% of oleyl amine. As shown in Fig. S3, it is found that the friction coefficient and wear volume value of oleyl amine with a concentration of 1.0 wt% are 0.0875 and 4.31 × 10−3 mm3 respectively, which is 22.9% and 45.6% better than pure PAO. Thus, the friction-reducing and antiwear properties of N-CDs originate from synergistic effect of oleyl amine and N-CDs. Importantly, the friction coefficients and wear volumes of PAO with hydrophobic N-CDs are also lower than that of PAO with 1.0 wt% content of ZDDP, which corresponds to reduce of 36.7% and 51.2% in maximum. Based on the above results, it is of great potential the hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs as nanoadditives in both polar and apolar base oils with high performance and environmentally friendly natures.

The N-CDs with appropriate concentration in both PEG and PAO systems is considered to form hybrid lubricating films between the surfaces of tribo-pairs owing to electrostatic adsorption thus contributing the sharp decrease of the friction curves at the early stage of tribology. With the increase of concentration, the N-CDs nano-additive can enter the rubbing surfaces, and as a result the lubrication effect appear to be significant. Thus, a lower friction coefficient of friction and smaller worn volume can be obtained [21, 22]. When the concentration increase over 1.0 wt% and the friction coefficient and worn volume slightly increase, which indicate the adhesive wear have occurred inducing by the aggregation of N-CDs between the interfaces and inhibit the continuous supplies of fine N-CDs to the contact zone for lubrication [10, 45].

Furthermore, testing of tribological properties of N-CDs in a commercial formulation would have been beneficial for industry. 5W-30 is a premium conventional engine oil designed to fight against sludge and engine wear, which offering proven protection to help extend engine life of gasoline-filled automobiles and light-duty trucks. In following test, we explored the tribological properties of hydrophobic N-CDs as additive in 5W-30 engine oil while 5W-30 engine oil as reference.

The wear volume and friction coefficient results lubricated with 5W-30 engine oil with and without hydrophobic N-CDs are shown in Fig. S3. 5W-30 engine oil have shown remarkable tribological properties whose friction coefficient and wear volume value are 0.089 and 0.43 × 10−3 mm−3 respectively. Tribological properties is raised to another higher stage when adding 1.0 wt% hydrophobic N-CDs in 5 W-30 engine oil with friction coefficient of 0.065 and wear volume of 0.29 × 10−3 mm3, which is 27.0% and 32.6% better than 5W-30 engine oil respectively. The results indicate that the N-CDs exhibit good compatibility with other constituents of the formulation thus contributing enhanced tribological properties.

In order to obtain more information about tribo-chemical reactions of hydrophobic N-CDs after test, it is essential to analyze the critical friction elements such as Fe, C, N and O on worn surfaces by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 11 ). In Fe2p XPS fine spectrum, it can be fitted into four peaks with the binding energies of 710.6, 719.7 and 724.4 eV, respectively. Contacting with the binding energy of 530.5 eV in O1s XPS fine spectrum, it can be identified as Fe2p3/2 for Fe, Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 for Fe2O3, respectively. The fine C1s spectrum can be fitted into three peak components with binding energies 284.6, 286.1 and 288.8 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CN and COH respectively. The fine N1s spectrum can be fitted into two peak components with binding energies at 401.7 and 398.9 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, suggesting that the additive and its decomposition products form an adsorption and tribo-reaction film on the worn surface. In addition, the peak of O1s with the binding energy of 531.1 eV corresponds to carboxylates [46, 47].

Micro-Raman spectroscopy was used to further identify the carbon forms on the worn surface (Fig. 12 ). It was found that there were several characteristic peaks of iron oxides below ~700 cm−1 lubricated by PAO, declaring oxidation process happening on the steel surface. For N-CDs lubricated surface, the spectrum detected carbon characteristic bands: D-band at ~1350 cm−1, G-band at ~1600 cm−1 and D + D' at ~2900 cm−1 [48]. And its value of ID/IG was calculated as 1.81, which is known as an estimation of the defect level. It suggested that carbon deposited on the worn surface with disorder and defect structure after adding N-CDs, which is consistent with results of EDS. Furthermore, peaks of iron oxides were relatively low lubricated with N-CDs, indicating that N-CDs can reduce the oxidation in a certain extent on the surface of steel balls during the friction.

The Raman mapping spectroscopy was used to further identify the carbon forms on the worn surface using the hydrophobic N-CDs in the PAO lube base oil. The Raman mapping image shows som03e distinct regions, which is clearly displayed by different color. The red color block (red line in Fig. 13 ) displayed the abundance of Raman active modes due to N-CDs deposited. However, a major area of worn scar (blue, black block and black line in Fig. 11) has no signatures of D bands at ~1350 cm−1, and G bands at ~1600 cm−1. Due to the roughness of contact interfaces and non-uniform load distribution, the N-CDs are not deposited uniformly on the worn scar, which primarily control the development of lubricant thin film. In this case, N-CDs exhibit self-mending effect by filling the deep scratches, which directly accounts for excellent anti-wear performances. Furthermore, the dispersed N-CDs in the PAO base oil easily sheared with weakly adhered N-CDs on the wear scar of steel ball and reduced the friction coefficient. The deposited N-CDs on the contact-interfaces could protect the tribo-pair and reduced the wear scar.

Based on the surface analysis by XPS, Raman and SEM-EDS, it could be inferred that the metal surface generated positive charges during the friction process due to the emission of low-energy electrons from the friction surface and then N-CDs with abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups could absorb onto the positive charged sites thus forming an adsorption film during the rigorous sliding process. Afterward, the N-CDs acted as bearing balls with rolling effect on the contact surface at initial stage, which significantly reduced friction. Because of the uneven load distribution and the roughness of the contact area, the N-CDs could deposit unevenly on the worn surface with mending effects as well. Followed, the intensive tribo-chemical reaction occurred and a tribo-chemical film containing Fe2O3, inorganic carbon and nitrogen element generated with the increase of the duration, which prevented direct contact of the metal surface and reduced the oxidation in a certain extent on the surface of steel balls during the friction. As a result, N-CDs as additive in base oils exhibited excellent friction-reducing and anti-wear performances.

In summary, a simple, economical, and eco-friendly approach was developed for preparation of two kind of fluorescent N-CDs with hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity. The hydrophilic N-CDs can be facilely tuned by surface modification, thus leading to the synthesis of hydrophobic N-CDs. Hydrophilic and hydrophobic N-CDs exhibited favorable solubility in various polar and apolar mediums, and their stable dispersions in PEG and PAO synthetic base oils were obtained for tribological evaluation. Both N-CDs enhanced the friction-reducing and antiwear properties of PEG and PAO base oils significantly under boundary lubrication. Noticeably, the hydrophilic N-CDs and hydrophobic N-CDs dispersions with low concentration displayed superior tribological properties in comparison with that of selected references such as bis(salicylato)borate ionic liquid (PEG) and the most common ZDDP anti-wear agent (PAO) under the same condition. It was found that tribo-chemical reactions occurred between N-CDs and the iron atoms/iron oxide species, leading to the formation of a boundary lubrication thin film which adhered on the steel rubbing surfaces and contributed to the lower friction and wear. Importantly, the N-CDs was adsorbed on the surface non-uniformly and demonstrated in this work, which could inhibit the oxidation in a certain extent on the surface of steel balls during the friction. These results are helpful not only in understanding of the lubrication mechanism of N-CDs, but also in designing new lubricating carbon dots nanoparticles that can meet emerging needs in practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21404114), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grand No. 2014M561797), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2015A610051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.06.042.
